textBoxes.text: Description: A 16 Hz quartz crystal drives a 74x393 counter that halves the frequency four times -- 8, 4, 2, then exactly 1 Hz -- the same way a quartz watch turns its crystal into seconds.

Details: The silver can is a 2-pin quartz crystal. A real crystal is a passive sliver of quartz -- it only oscillates with an amplifier (usually an inverter) wrapped around it. This simulator idealizes it as a ready-made clock: pin A outputs a square wave at the can's fixed frequency (50% duty cycle) and pin B is its ground leg. A crystal's frequency is fixed by its quartz cut -- in real life you change it by swapping the can, and that is exactly what right-clicking the crystal models: pick a different can (or type a custom frequency). Duty stays locked at 50%. The red LED hangs straight off the crystal: at 16 Hz it flickers too fast to follow. The 74x393's first counter divides by 2 at every stage: QA blinks at 8 Hz, QB at 4 Hz, QC at 2 Hz, and QD (blue) at exactly 1 Hz -- one blink per second, derived purely from the crystal. A quartz watch does the identical trick: a 32,768 Hz crystal divided by two 15 times gives the 1 Hz that steps the second hand. Wiring notes: the counter advances on falling clock edges; CLR1 is tied LOW so counter 1 runs free, and the unused second counter is parked (CLR2 tied HIGH holds it cleared, CLK2 tied LOW).
